By Shawn Twing
The wild west days of Internet marketing are over. Tricks don’t
work. Flavor of the day, too good to be true “secret” tactics don’t
exist.
If someone is selling the “how to get rich with Google/Facebook
top secret approach” that’s a good sign that whatever they’re
selling is about �fteen minutes away from not working and/or
getting your account banned.
Make no mistake, this is very good news.
Trying to stay one step ahead of Google and Facebook is a road to
nowhere.
We’re going to do exactly the opposite — we’re going to give
Google and Facebook exactly what they want (and exactly what
their audiences want).
The best part?
This is actually easier and produces WAY better results than
Give ‘Em What They Want (And
Get What You Want)
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 9 --

trying to game the system.
Start With the Basics
Let’s start with the basics.
What is the #1 thing that both Google and Facebook need?
You guessed it — money.
Yes, Google is a search engine, and yes, Facebook is a social media
platform. However, what pays for the employees, the fancy
buildings, and all the tech? Ad revenue. Google earns nearly 90%
of all of its revenue from advertising. Facebook is closer to 98%.
Let that sink in for a minute.
Google and Facebook WANT and NEED your money. They
don’t exist without advertising revenue. There’s no need to trick
them into letting you advertise on their networks. If anything,
they want to HELP you spend your money with them.
However, there is a catch.
Google and Facebook don’t exist without their enormous base of
users.
No users means no revenue opportunities. Both are
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 9 --

fundamentally in the business of selling user attention to
advertisers, and that means they need to keep their users happy
and engaged. That also means that you, as an advertiser, need to
keep their users happy and engaged.
How do you do that?
By giving each platform’s users what they want.
What Users Really Want
Let’s talk about what that means and especially the differences
between the two.
Google is �rst and foremost a search engine. Google doesn’t really
do anything until we ask it a question.
"How do I ..."
"Where do I ..."
"What's the best way to ..."
You get the idea.
Think about it for a moment.
In fact, take a moment to go look at Google.com right now.
What is there to do?
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 9 --

The short answer — not much. Unless I bring a question to
Google, it doesn’t really serve any purpose. I can’t browse Google. I
can’t see what my friends are doing on Google. Everything I do
with Google begins with me.
Facebook, on the other hand, is personal space. It’s where we post
pics of our kids, check in on what our friends are doing, virtue
signal, and follow people, entities, and ideas that interest us.
We browse Facebook — we don’t search.
Our 100% custom, made-for-us Facebook news feed exists the
moment we open the app 24/7/365, and it is designed to capture
and hold our attention without requiring us to do anything except
scroll.
Google requires effort.
We want something, need to know something, need a solution for
something. When the roof is leaking at 3:00 a.m. we turn to
Google to �nd “emergency roof repair near me”. When the kids
need help with homework, we turn to Google to learn “how to
solve for X”.
When was the last time you took a break at work to go browse
Google?
Facebook is designed to be effortless. Just click the icon and
everything is done for you. Pics of your friends having a drink on
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 9 --

the beach in Belize — check. Snarky political memes for a quick
laugh — Check. The latest irritating humble-brag from a frenemy
— check.
When was the last time you went to Facebook to solve a problem
(other than boredom)?
Both Google and Facebook want positive experiences for their
users. Google is all about search, which means Google wants it
users to �nd what they’re looking for. Google wouldn’t be Google
if you searched for one thing and found something else.
Google relies on its users to con�rm that they found what they
were looking for.
When you search for something, click on a link, and hit your back
button immediately, Google is watching, analyzing, and assessing.
When you search for something, click on a link, and browse
through ten pages of content for thirty minutes, Google sees that
too.
If those two links were from paid ads, which do you think Google
sees as more relevant and bene�cial to its users — the ten-page,
30-minute version, of course.
The AdWords platform re�ects Google’s focus on user experience
by assessing a 1-10 number called Quality Score to every keyword
in an advertising account.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 9 --

Quality Score affects the price you pay for a click, and the position
in which your ad appears. Higher quality scores often mean better
ad positions for a lower cost per click.
Think about that for a moment — Google rewards you, the
advertiser, with lower advertising costs and better ad position
when you create a better experience for its users. (We’ll get into
the speci�cs of how to do that in lesson 4.)
Facebook’s advertising platform assigns a relevance score to its
ads.
Also a number 1-10, relevance score lets you know how well you’ve
matched your message to your audience.
Leave the same ads in rotation to the same audience for a long
time and you’ll see your relevance scores fall. Why? Facebook
wants to entertain its users and keep them engaged in their feed.
That means new, novel, entertaining, curiosity-inspiring content
is king.
The multi-page pre-sell site is the PERFECT strategy for
Facebook advertising, especially when it’s framed with curiosity
and intrigue (more on that later).
Quick Recap
Let’s recap where we are.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 9 --

Our goal is to give Google and Facebook what they want which
means high-quality, interesting answers to questions (Google),
and entertaining/informing/intriguing content for Facebook.
When we do that, Google and Facebook will reward us with
lower advertising costs and better positioning and more
frequent display of our advertising.
NOTE: One last thing to cover before we move on. Google and
Facebook have rules, and it’s important to understand (and follow)
those rules. If you’re in a market or have a business model that Google
or Facebook don’t like, there’s no long-term strategy for gaming the
system.
Read the rules before you invest a lot of time.
I had an experience recently working with a business that had
invested tens of thousands of dollars to create an online store only to
�nd that their product could not be advertised on Google or Facebook.
That was an expensive lesson to learn, and one that could have been
avoided with a couple of hours of research. They’re stuck now with an
online business and no reliable way to generate tra�c. Don’t let that
be you.
Here’s a list of resources I recommend:
▪ Google AdWords Policies
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 9 --

▪ Google Webmaster Guidelines (read the quality guidelines and
basic principles)
▪ Facebook Advertising Policies
www.getwsodo.com

www.getwsodo.com
